---
title: "Ordinal Analysis"
author: Simmons & Girard
date: March 18, 2026
theme: lumen
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
---


# 1. Prep Data

## 1.1. Libraries
```{r, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
library(simhelpers)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
```

## 1.2. Directory & Loading Data

```{r}
DIR <- "~/Documents/Github/MastersThesis/iccs/data"
dat_nest <- readRDS(paste0(DIR,'/Ordinal_run1_full.rds'))
#head(dat_nest) 

#Kable (doesn't render?)
# k1 <- kable(dat_nest, format = "html") %>%
#   kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover")) %>%
#   scroll_box(width = "100%", height = "200%")
# k1 
```

## 1.3. Data Wrangling 
```{r}
## unnest the results' list-column  (by hand; example with "icc" column which is numeric) 

# dat <- dat_nest |> 
#   mutate(result = map(result, as_tibble)) |>
#   unnest(result) |>
#   mutate(
#     icc = map_dbl(icc, \(x) pluck(x, .default= NA_real_))) #example

##  unnest efficiently, let R decide the best atomic data type
dat <- dat_nest |> 
  mutate(result = map(result, as_tibble)) |>
  unnest(result) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.list), \(col) {
    map_vec(col, \(x) if(is.null(x)) NA else x)
  }))

#set conditions as factors
conds <- c("n_raters", "n_objects", "k_category", "e_category")
dat <- dat |> 
  mutate(across(.cols = all_of(conds), .fns = as.factor))

# Delete nested object
rm(dat_nest)

# Print
head(dat)

```

<b> In the ordinal case, the parameter grid of conditions is as follows: </b>

```{r}
design_factors <- list(
n_raters = c(3,12,24),
n_objects = c(10,50,100),
target_icc = c(0.40,0.60,0.80),
k_category = c(3,5,7),
e_category = c(TRUE,FALSE) # 1 = equal category prevalence, #0 = unequal linear decay prevalence 
)
#expand grid
params <- expand_grid( !!!design_factors) 
design_factors
head(params)
```


# 2. Performance Measures for Point Estimators
Here, we consider measures for point estimators for each of the 162 ordinal conditions

  - Bias and Standard Errors in Estimators

```{r}
bias_estimators <- dat |>
  #group by each condition (For ordinal, 162 total conditions)
  select(!c(seed, filename)) |>
  group_by(n_raters, n_objects, target_icc, k_category, e_category) |> 
  summarize(
    mean_icc_hat = mean(icc, na.rm = TRUE), #across 1000 iterations of simulation
    se_icc_hat = sd(icc, na.rm = TRUE), # SD of sampling dist. is SE
    dropped_icc = sum(is.na(icc))/sum(!is.na(icc)) *100,
    mean_gICC_hat = mean(g_icc, na.rm = TRUE),#across 1000 iterations of simulation
    se_gICC_hat = sd(g_icc, na.rm = TRUE), #SD of sampling dist. is SE
    dropped_gICC = sum(is.na(g_icc))/sum(!is.na(g_icc)) * 100) |>
  # let's number each condition/case for simplicity
  ungroup()|>
  mutate(CaseNumber = row_number()) |>
  mutate(
    target_icc = as.numeric(target_icc),
    bias_icc_hat = mean_icc_hat - target_icc,
    #compare gICC to benchmark of generalized ICC of same condition
    #this assumes the gICC is more accurate than the ICC 
    per_SE_icc_hat = se_icc_hat / se_gICC_hat, 
    bias_gICC_hat = mean_gICC_hat - target_icc,
    per_SE_gICC_hat = se_gICC_hat/se_gICC_hat)
    
bias_estimators
```

## 2.1 Discussion: Point Estimates Bias
It appears that the traditional ICC (using \{lme4\}) is systematically negatively biased in all conditions from the true ICC value from -18% (Case #14) to -0.04% (Case #24) in ICC units. 
```{r}
range(bias_estimators$bias_icc_hat)
rbind(bias_estimators[14,], #-18.6
      bias_estimators[24,]) #-0.04$ 
```

It appears that the generalized ICC (using \{ordinal\}) is biased from -7.01% to -1.44% (in ICC units), but note that these are in cases when there is only 3 raters! 

```{r}
range(bias_estimators$bias_gICC_hat)
rbind(bias_estimators[1,], #-18.6
      bias_estimators[48,]) #-0.04$ 

```
  
  - Root Mean Square Error of Estimators 
  
```{r}
RMSE_estimators <- dat |>
  #group by each condition (For ordinal, 162 total conditions)
  select(!c(seed, filename)) |>
  group_by(n_raters, n_objects, target_icc, k_category, e_category) |> 
  summarize(
    bias_icc_hat = mean(icc - target_icc, na.rm = TRUE),
    icc_SE = sd(icc, na.rm = TRUE),
    icc_RMSE = sqrt(mean(icc-target_icc, na.rm=TRUE)^2),
    bias_gICC_hat = mean(g_icc - target_icc, na.rm =TRUE), 
    gICC_SE = sd(g_icc, na.rm = TRUE), 
    gICC_RMSE = sqrt(mean(g_icc - target_icc, na.rm = TRUE)^2)
  ) |> ungroup() |>
  mutate(
    CaseNumber = row_number()
  )
  
RMSE_estimators
```
